_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan Part B (paragraph matching), REWORKED to template length 450-650 words. 7 labelled paragraphs (A-G), each developed to 5-7 sentences; 5 statements per passage are strong paraphrases (not literal lifts) matching one distinct paragraph. answers = 0-based paragraph indices; paras=7. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage:
[A] For most of us, failure is something to be hidden. From an early age we are taught, at school and at home, to display our successes prominently and to bury our mistakes as quickly and quietly as possible. A good report card is pinned to the wall; a poor one vanishes into a drawer. In most areas of ordinary life this instinct does little harm. In science, however, where genuine progress depends on knowing precisely what does not work, the very same habit can prove surprisingly damaging, quietly distorting the record on which sound discovery is built.

[B] Consider one marine biologist who, frustrated by this culture of concealment, began posting a public 'failure log', setting down in plain detail every experiment that had gone wrong and every promising idea that had collapsed. Her senior colleagues were baffled, and a few were faintly embarrassed on her behalf. Yet young researchers from across the world wrote to thank her. Seeing that an accomplished scientist also stumbled, repeatedly and openly, gave them the courage to carry on after their own disheartening setbacks.

[C] The harm done by hidden failure is subtle but real. When beginners are shown only the polished, published triumphs of others, they understandably conclude that everyone else finds research smooth and effortless, and that their own ceaseless struggles mark them out as uniquely unfit for the work. The conclusion is false, but it is also corrosive, and many gifted newcomers, quietly persuaded that they do not belong, abandon the field long before they would otherwise have succeeded.

[D] Failure also carries a deeper scientific value that the habit of concealment obscures. Confirming that a result can be reproduced by other hands, and carefully recording the many attempts that cannot, lies at the very centre of reliable knowledge. A negative result, far from being wasted labour, does useful work of its own: it narrows the field of remaining possibilities and warns later investigators away from a path that has already been shown to lead nowhere.

[E] Yet the wider system frequently works against such openness. Academic journals overwhelmingly favour positive, novel and eye-catching findings, leaving little space for the patient, unglamorous reporting of what simply did not happen. Researchers, who must publish in order to survive professionally, soon absorb the unspoken lesson that failure, however instructive it may be, does nothing to advance a career, and they shape their conduct accordingly.

[F] Some newer practices, however, are beginning to shift these incentives. 'Registered reports', for example, allow a study's question and method to be reviewed and accepted before any results are known, so that the work is assured of publication whatever its eventual outcome. By rewarding rigorous design rather than exciting conclusions, such arrangements strip away much of the temptation to conceal, ignore or quietly massage disappointing data.

[G] None of this romanticises failure or pretends that being wrong is in any way pleasant. The point is simply that a science able to speak only of its victories understands itself poorly and misleads those who follow. Learning to discuss failure openly, honestly and without shame may, in the end, be one of the most rigorous and trustworthy things that any community of researchers can bring itself to do.
statements: Because journals reward striking, original outcomes, scientists are dissuaded from candidly setting down their unsuccessful attempts.
Watching an established researcher openly admit her own repeated setbacks gave less experienced colleagues the resolve to keep going.
Under a recent reviewing scheme, a paper's acceptance is secured before anyone yet knows whether its findings will be positive or negative.
When only the successes of others are on view, novices mistakenly suspect that their own difficulties are theirs alone.
Checking that findings can be repeated, and documenting those that cannot, is fundamental to dependable science.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(期刊偏好正面/新颖结果,使人不愿如实记录失败) S2→B(资深科学家公开承认失败,鼓励后辈坚持) S3→F(注册报告:结果未知前即定录用) S4→C(只见他人成功,新人误以为只有自己受挫) S5→D(可复现+记录失败是可靠科学根本)。A/G 未匹配。强同义改写,非字面照搬。

sets.passage:
[A] After several decades in which cities were comprehensively reshaped around the private car, urban planners are rediscovering a surprisingly old idea: that the finest way to move through a city may simply be on foot. What once looked like mere nostalgia is now backed by a growing body of careful evidence, and the humble pedestrian has returned to the very centre of serious thinking about how human settlements ought to be built and rebuilt.

[B] The benefits begin with ordinary health. Residents of genuinely walkable neighbourhoods take substantially more physical exercise across a typical day, almost without noticing the effort, simply because shops, schools, clinics and parks all lie within easy and pleasant reach. Movement is woven naturally into daily routine rather than set aside as a separate chore to be squeezed, with reluctance, into an already crowded schedule.

[C] Walkability also quietly supports local commerce. When people stroll rather than drive, they pass dozens of small shops, stalls and cafes at a relaxed human pace, and stop on impulse in a way that a motorist speeding toward a distant shopping mall never does. The street itself becomes a kind of marketplace, and modest independent businesses, which depend heavily on this passing trade, find the steady stream of customers they need to survive.

[D] There are environmental gains as well. Short journeys completed on foot directly replace trips that would otherwise have been made by car, cutting both the emissions those journeys release and the demand for the vast, land-hungry expanses of parking that car-based cities inevitably require. The savings from any single walk are tiny, but a neighbourhood where most errands can be done on foot quietly removes dozens of short drives from each family's week, and multiplied across a whole population the effect is far from trivial.

[E] Critics warn, however, that making a district attractive carries its own risk. As a neighbourhood becomes more desirable, rents tend to climb steadily, and the rising cost can push out precisely the long-standing residents who gave the place its distinctive character in the first place. Walkability, they insist, must be deliberately paired with genuinely affordable housing if it is not, in the end, to defeat its own humane purpose.

[F] Designing well for walking, moreover, involves far more than simply laying down pavements. It calls for a fine grain of interconnected streets, a rich mixture of uses within easy reach, shade and shelter from harsh weather, and an unmistakable sense of safety that quietly invites people to slow down, linger and meet one another rather than hurry anxiously through. Streets that achieve this feel less like corridors built for traffic and more like shared outdoor rooms in which the everyday life of a community can openly unfold.

[G] The car, of course, will not vanish, and few sensible planners wish it to. But there is a growing acceptance that a city built solely and single-mindedly for driving is, in a deep and important sense, a city that has forgotten the very people it exists to serve, and that remembering them again begins with something as modest as the pavement underfoot.
statements: Measures that increase a district's appeal can raise rents and force out the residents who shaped its character.
People living in walkable areas accumulate more exercise as a natural by-product of everyday life.
Passing small shops on foot prompts the kind of spontaneous buying that fast car travel never encourages.
Substituting walking for short car trips lowers both emissions and the need for parking.
Effective pedestrian design rests on connected streets, mixed uses and an evident feeling of safety.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(吸引力上升推高租金,挤走塑造特色的居民) S2→B(可步行区居民日常自然锻炼更多) S3→C(步行经过小店促成冲动消费) S4→D(短途步行替代开车,减排+减停车需求) S5→F(好的步行设计依赖连贯街道/混合功能/安全感)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] A decade ago, a great many commentators confidently predicted that the public library would soon become obsolete. With the whole of human knowledge apparently migrating online, who, they asked, would still bother to visit a draughty building full of paper? And yet, in city after city, libraries today are busier than they have ever been, their reading rooms full, their events oversubscribed and their membership rising steadily rather than falling away.

[B] Part of the explanation is plain access. Not everyone owns a computer or enjoys a fast and reliable connection at home, and for these residents, who are often among the poorest, the library remains an essential gateway to information, to official services and to the simple act of learning. Far from being made redundant by the internet, the library has become one of the very few places where reliable access to it is genuinely free and open to all.

[C] Libraries have also evolved into genuine community spaces. Students gather to study together at long shared tables; parents bring small children to weekly story hours; and those who live alone discover, in a warm and undemanding public room, somewhere simply to be among other human beings without any obligation to spend money. For a newcomer who knows no one, or an elderly resident living by themselves, that quiet, unquestioned permission to sit among others can be precious, a small daily defence against the loneliness that large cities so easily breed.

[D] Their practical role, too, has widened far beyond lending novels. Many branches now lend out e-readers, hand tools, musical instruments and even small packets of seeds, and run free classes in subjects ranging from computer coding to the rights and duties of citizenship. The library has quietly become a place to acquire useful skills as much as a place to borrow objects, adapting its services to whatever its particular community most needs.

[E] This steady evolution has not been free of tension. Some observers argue that libraries ought to concentrate strictly on books and reading, the work they were originally founded to do, while others insist with equal conviction that responding flexibly to the real and changing needs of a community is precisely the mission a modern library should embrace. The dispute is, at bottom, a disagreement about what a library is fundamentally for.

[F] Funding, meanwhile, remains perpetually fragile. Because the benefits a library provides are spread thinly and quietly across an entire population rather than concentrated in any single vocal group, the institution is politically easy to cut and frustratingly hard to defend when difficult choices must be made around a tight municipal budget.

[G] What endures through all these changes is a quietly radical idea: a genuinely public space, open without charge to absolutely everyone, that asks for nothing in return but a flicker of curiosity. It is a rare institution that judges no visitor and turns no one away, and in an age that prices almost everything, that idea may be more valuable, and more worth protecting, than ever.
statements: Libraries are politically easy to reduce because the good they do is shared widely and quietly rather than felt sharply by any one group.
For those who lack their own devices and connections, the library is a crucial means of reaching information.
Opinion is divided over whether libraries should confine themselves to books or respond to wider community needs.
Beyond lending books, libraries now circulate varied items and offer instruction in many skills.
The library provides a shared room where people may study together or merely be in others' company.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(受益广而薄,预算中易被砍难被护) S2→B(无设备/网络者获取信息的关键入口) S3→E(只做书 vs 回应社区需求之争) S4→D(借多样物品+教多样技能) S5→C(共享空间:一起学习或与人相处)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] We habitually think of sleep as time switched off, a passive and slightly wasteful pause inserted between the active waking hours that supposedly matter. It can feel like a nightly surrender, a long stretch of nothingness we would gladly shorten if only we could find a way. Research accumulated over recent decades, however, tells a strikingly different and far more interesting story about what the sleeping brain is in fact doing while the body lies still.

[B] During sleep, the brain is remarkably busy. Far from idling, it methodically replays the experiences of the preceding day, and in doing so strengthens and stabilises the delicate neural connections that encode our newly formed memories. Patterns of electrical activity sweep across the sleeping brain in carefully ordered stages, each thought to play its own distinct part in this nightly labour, so that the work of fixing what we have learned is carried out largely while we lie apparently inert in the dark.

[C] This hidden labour is precisely why sleep is so intimately bound up with learning. Students who sleep soon after studying reliably remember far more of the material than those who instead stay awake, however determinedly the sleepless ones go on revising. A single night of proper rest, in this sense, does more for lasting retention than several extra hours of anxious, bleary-eyed effort late into the night.

[D] Sleep also performs a kind of nightly cleaning. A specialised system within the brain, most active in the small hours, flushes away the metabolic by-products that steadily accumulate during the day's waking thought, clearing residues that might otherwise build up and interfere with healthy function over the longer term. Sleep, in other words, is not merely rest but active maintenance, as essential to the brain as regular cleaning is to any machine that runs all day.

[E] The costs of going without are correspondingly severe. Chronic short sleep has been linked not merely to poor concentration and low mood but to a weakened immune system and a measurably higher risk of serious long-term illness. The body, denied its nightly maintenance, pays the bill slowly but surely, and studies tracking people over many years have repeatedly tied prolonged poor sleep to outcomes that no one would willingly choose.

[F] Modern life, unfortunately, treats sleep as eminently expendable, something to be sacrificed without a second thought for the sake of work or entertainment, and it is even, perversely, worn as a badge of dedication and toughness. To boast of needing little sleep has become, in some circles, a strange point of pride; few would brag of skipping meals for a week, yet skipping sleep is somehow quietly admired.

[G] The science offers a blunt and unwelcome correction to that attitude. Sleep is not the mere absence of productivity, a gap to be minimised wherever possible; it is, on the contrary, one of productivity's hidden foundations, and a culture that despises it is quietly undermining the very performance and health it claims to prize.
statements: Society frequently treats sleep as something that can be given up, even flaunting the sacrifice as proof of commitment.
As we sleep, the brain actively consolidates the memories laid down during the day.
Those who sleep after studying hold on to more than those who simply stay up revising.
A process operating at night clears away the waste that gathers during waking thought.
Persistent shortage of sleep damages concentration, weakens immunity and threatens long-term health.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(把睡眠当可牺牲品,甚至炫耀为奉献) S2→B(睡眠中大脑巩固当天记忆) S3→C(学后睡比熬夜复习记得多) S4→D(夜间过程清除清醒思考的废物) S5→E(长期缺觉损害专注/免疫/长期健康)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Human attention has quietly become one of the most valuable commodities of the present age. Whole industries now compete, second by second and screen by screen, to capture it, hold it and resell it, and a great deal of ingenuity is devoted to the single goal of keeping our eyes fixed a little longer than we ever intended. What feels, from the inside, like idle and harmless browsing is, from the other side of the screen, a carefully engineered and fiercely fought contest for our minds.

[B] The underlying business model is disarmingly simple. A large number of apparently free services actually earn their money by keeping users engaged for as long as humanly possible, and then selling that accumulated attention, neatly packaged and sorted, to advertisers willing to pay handsomely for it. The user, in the now-familiar phrase, is not really the customer at all but the product being quietly sold.

[C] To achieve this, such services deliberately exploit well-known weaknesses of the human mind. Endless scrolling feeds that never reach a satisfying bottom, unpredictable rewards that arrive at random intervals, and a steady drip of notifications are all carefully engineered to be difficult to put down, borrowing the very same psychological tricks that make gambling so compulsive. The result is that setting the phone aside requires a constant and tiring act of will that the design itself is built to defeat.

[D] The personal costs of all this are steadily mounting. Many people now report a noticeably fractured capacity for deep and sustained focus, finding it harder than they once did to read a long book from start to finish or to follow an extended and demanding argument without reaching, almost reflexively, for a glowing screen. Powers of patience and concentration once taken entirely for granted increasingly feel like muscles that have quietly wasted away through simple disuse.

[E] There are broader social costs as well. When platforms systematically reward whatever provokes the strongest and quickest emotional reaction, calm, patient and complex discussion is steadily crowded out by outrage and crude oversimplification, and public conversation grows coarser and more combative as a direct result. Measured purely by engagement, a furious quarrel counts as a triumph, while a thoughtful exchange that leaves everyone calmer is, by the same cold measure, a failure.

[F] Some people are beginning to push back, deliberately reclaiming their attention through conscious effort. They set aside quiet hours free of all devices, delete the most demanding applications from their phones, and even allow themselves simply to be bored, rediscovering in the process the quiet but considerable value of an undivided and unhurried mind.

[G] Attention, in the final analysis, is no trivial or inexhaustible resource. How we choose to spend it, hour by hour and year by year, shapes in very large part the kind of people we gradually become, so that to decide what deserves our attention is, increasingly, to decide who we are.
statements: By favouring whatever triggers the fiercest reaction, platforms squeeze out calm and complicated discussion.
Ostensibly free services make money by retaining users' attention and selling it on to advertisers.
Devices such as bottomless feeds are purpose-built to be hard for users to stop using.
Many report that their capacity for sustained, deep concentration has noticeably weakened.
Some people intentionally recover their focus by removing apps and tolerating boredom.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(奖励最激烈反应,挤走理性复杂讨论) S2→B(免费服务靠留住注意力卖广告) S3→C(无限信息流被刻意设计成难停下) S4→D(深度专注能力明显被削弱) S5→F(主动夺回专注:删App/容忍无聊)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Some of the most useful scientific data gathered in recent years has come not from salaried professionals but from ordinary volunteers, in a fast-growing movement that has come to be known as citizen science. Equipped with little more than a smartphone and a measure of enthusiasm, members of the public are now contributing to genuine research on a scale that would, until very recently, have seemed wholly unimaginable.

[B] The appeal to professional researchers is obvious once it is plainly stated. Thousands of amateur observers, spread across an entire continent and active every day of the year, can together record bird sightings, rainfall or water quality on a scale and at a level of detail that no conventionally funded team, however well resourced, could ever hope to match alone. Birds crossing a whole continent, or pollution drifting slowly along a river, can in truth only be followed when many eyes are watching at once.

[C] The volunteers, for their part, gain a great deal in return. Taking part can deepen a person's understanding of the natural world, foster a satisfying sense of contributing to something larger than oneself, and transform what might have been an aimless weekend walk into a purposeful and absorbing act of genuine discovery. Many describe a quiet thrill on realising that a single observation, jotted down on an ordinary afternoon, has become a permanent part of the scientific record.

[D] Doubts about the quality of the resulting data were, understandably, once widespread. Sceptical professionals asked, not unreasonably, whether observations collected by untrained hands, with varying care and inconsistent equipment, could really be trusted enough to support serious scientific conclusions at all.

[E] Careful design, however, has answered much of that worry. Clear and simple instructions, well-chosen tools, and built-in cross-checks that automatically flag improbable entries together allow very large volunteer datasets to reach standards that compare favourably with those of professional surveys. What looks at first like an unavoidable weakness, the unevenness of amateur effort, can be largely corrected by intelligent design rather than by shutting amateurs out altogether.

[F] The approach nevertheless has real limits that its keenest enthusiasts sometimes overlook. It works best for tasks that are straightforward to explain and to observe with the naked eye, and considerably less well for those that demand specialised equipment, lengthy training or fine expert judgement that cannot easily be reduced to a simple printed rule.

[G] At its best, though, citizen science accomplishes far more than the mere accumulation of numbers in a database. It quietly helps to rebuild the frayed and often distrustful link between the general public and the scientific research that increasingly shapes their world, for people who have helped to gather the evidence themselves are far likelier to trust the conclusions later drawn from it.
statements: Thoughtfully written instructions and verification checks allow amateur observations to match professional standards.
Researchers prize the enormous scale of observation that large numbers of volunteers make achievable.
Participants themselves acquire understanding and a rewarding sense of meaningful involvement.
People once questioned whether data gathered by untrained volunteers could be relied upon.
The method is better suited to easily observed tasks than to those requiring specialised expertise.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(清晰说明+交叉核对让业余数据达专业标准) S2→B(研究者看重志愿者带来的大规模观测) S3→C(参与者获理解与贡献感) S4→D(曾质疑未受训志愿者数据可信度) S5→F(更适合易观察任务,不适合需专业判断者)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] In an age dominated by fast food and sprawling global supply chains, a quiet counter-movement has emerged to urge us to slow down and eat, as far as possible, what grows nearby. Its champions present local eating not as a passing fashion but as a more thoughtful and grounded way of relating both to the food on our plates and to the land and people that actually produce it.

[B] Its supporters point first of all to taste. Food eaten close to where it was grown can be picked riper, because it does not have to survive a long and bruising journey, and it arrives fresher on the table; for this simple reason it often tastes noticeably better than equivalent produce shipped half-way around the world and ripened artificially somewhere in transit.

[C] There is an environmental argument too, frequently and confidently made. Shorter journeys from field to plate can, in principle, mean fewer transport emissions and less wasteful packaging, although, as careful studies repeatedly show, the full calculation is a good deal more complicated, and less flattering to local food, than its keenest advocates tend to assume. Transport, it turns out, is often only a small share of a food's total environmental cost, much of which is fixed long before the journey home ever begins.

[D] Eating locally can also strengthen the fabric of communities. Money spent with nearby growers tends to stay and circulate within the region rather than draining away to distant shareholders, and the practice helps rebuild direct, human ties between the people who grow food and the people who actually eat it. A farmers' market, on this view, is not merely a place to shop but a small weekly act of rebuilding trust between town and surrounding countryside.

[E] Yet the movement faces fair and serious criticism. A diet restricted strictly to local produce can be markedly more expensive than the ordinary supermarket alternative, and in many large cities and harsher climates it is simply impossible to sustain for much of the year without considerable hardship and a very narrow, monotonous plate. Insisting on it everywhere would condemn many city dwellers to food both dull and dear.

[F] Critics further point out that 'local' is not always the same thing as 'green', however casually the two are equated. A crop raised in an intensively heated greenhouse just down the road may, once its heavy energy use is honestly counted, pollute considerably more than the very same crop grown in an open field on the far side of the world.

[G] Perhaps the wisest conclusion, then, is one of balance rather than dogma: to value and choose local food wherever doing so genuinely makes sense, while refusing to treat sheer physical distance as the single, overriding measure of what we ought, responsibly, to eat. Distance is one consideration among several, not a commandment to be obeyed at any cost.
statements: Purchasing food from nearby producers keeps money circulating locally and restores direct links between growers and eaters.
Crops grown close by can be harvested riper and fresher and frequently taste better.
Limiting oneself to locally grown food can prove expensive and unworkable in many settings.
Cutting transport distance may lower emissions, though the complete picture is more complicated.
Food produced nearby is not automatically gentler on the environment.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(本地消费留住资金+重建产销人际联系) S2→B(就近采摘更熟更鲜更好吃) S3→E(只吃本地昂贵且许多地方不可行) S4→C(缩短运输或减排但全局更复杂) S5→F(本地未必更环保:温室耗能)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] The comfortable idea that a person's education ends, once and for all, with the award of a diploma is rapidly becoming obsolete. As technologies and entire industries are remade and overturned within the span of a single working career, learning can no longer be merely a phase to be completed in youth and then set aside; it must instead become a lifelong habit, returned to again and again as circumstances shift beneath our feet.

[B] The pressure is felt most sharply in the world of work. Skills that all but guaranteed a secure and well-paid job a decade ago can lose much of their value with startling speed, and workers across many fields are now expected to retrain not merely once but several times over the course of their lives, often in directions they could not possibly have foreseen. A worker who trained once, in youth, and assumed that this would last a lifetime increasingly belongs to a vanishing world.

[C] Employers, too, share a substantial part of this burden, whether they welcome it or not. Companies that neglect to help their staff keep their skills current risk seeing those valuable people leave for better-supported rivals, and they then discover how costly and uncertain it can be to recruit ready-made talent from an open market that everyone else is chasing at exactly the same moment.

[D] Technology offers at least part of the solution to the very problem it has helped create. A vast and growing array of online courses now places expert instruction within reach of almost anyone who has a decent internet connection, frequently at very low cost or none at all, dissolving the barriers of geography and price that once shut most ordinary people out. A curious adult in a remote town can now study under teachers who, only a generation ago, would have been wholly beyond reach.

[E] Access, however, remains stubbornly uneven. Those who possess the least time, money and quiet confidence, and who would in fact gain the most from retraining, are too often precisely the people least able, in practice, to take such opportunities up, so that the very tools meant to widen learning can quietly deepen the divides that already exist.

[F] Motivation matters at least as much as access. Adults, research consistently finds, learn most effectively when their study connects clearly and concretely to goals they themselves genuinely hold, rather than being imposed from above as an empty box-ticking requirement. Learning freely chosen, in the service of a real ambition, succeeds where learning merely required so often quietly fails.

[G] A society that learns continuously is, on the whole, both more adaptable in the face of relentless change and more humane in spirit. The central challenge, never quite solved, is to ensure that the genuine opportunity to keep learning is made truly and equally open to all, and not reserved, in practice, for those already most advantaged.
statements: The very people who stand to benefit most from retraining frequently have the least opportunity to undertake it.
Abilities that once secured good employment can rapidly lose their worth.
Businesses that overlook their workers' development risk losing them and then face costly recruitment.
Online courses deliver expert teaching to almost anyone, commonly at little or no cost.
Adults learn most effectively when their study plainly connects to goals of their own.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(最该再培训者最难获得机会,反而加深鸿沟) S2→B(曾保证好工作的技能迅速贬值) S3→C(忽视员工发展的公司流失人才+招聘成本高) S4→D(在线课程低/零成本普及专家教学) S5→F(学习贴合自身目标时最有效)。A/G 未匹配。
